lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufactured-home dealers that repeatedly serve community operators, developers, government or institutional buyers, or provide repeat outsourced coordination of home selection, permitting, financing, transport, installation, site preparation and post-sale service to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure one-time consumer product retail, manufacturer captive outlets without a transferable external service operation, parts-only stores, manufacturers, community ownership, construction contractors outside the dealer, shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B home sales and dealer-coordination services for community operators and developers, plus service-rich retail transactions where dealer margin and ancillary fees compensate selection, financing, permitting, delivery, installation and site coordination.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS code is primarily durable-goods retail and therefore mixes one-time consumer sellers with repeat outsourced dealer-coordinators. The lens is narrowed to repeat B2B and service-rich dealer operations so the screened population satisfies the recurring or repeat outsourced-service requirement.

a.rationale: Dealer labor includes lead qualification, configuration and quoting, marketing, CRM follow-up, document collection, finance packaging, contract preparation, order tracking, permitting packets and scheduling. O*NET shows both retail-selling work and highly structured loan-document work that AI can assist or substitute. Site assessment, complex customer counseling, negotiations, inspections, transport and installation oversight remain human- and field-intensive.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary from 20-30 independent and regional US dealers in the target EBITDA band, separated among sales, finance, permitting, installation coordination, warranty, administration and field roles, with current automation marked.
caveats: O*NET profiles retail sales and lending occupations generally, not a measured occupation mix for manufactured-home dealers.
The estimate excludes work already automated and does not treat regulated approval or physical field coordination as fully substitutable.
The injected labor ratio is measured at ancestor 44-45 with mismatched 2024 wage and 2022 receipt vintages, so it may poorly represent service-rich dealerships.

rho.rationale: Structured sales, finance, permit and closing documents create a practical five-year implementation path for intake, extraction, validation, drafting, reminders and workflow routing. Implementation is limited by state and local rule variation, lender and manufacturer system integration, privacy, fair-lending controls, error liability, low dealer IT maturity and required human accountability for financing and site readiness.
rho.change_evidence: A controlled multi-dealer pilot measuring cycle time, labor hours, exception rates, fair-lending review findings, permit rejections and closing errors for AI-assisted lead-to-installation workflows over at least two selling seasons.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in this dealer industry.
The service-rich firms retained by the lens may be more operationally complex than the average retail outlet.

e.rationale: The Census definition covers establishments retailing new or used manufactured homes, parts and equipment, so most firms are product retailers rather than recurring outsourced-service providers. The eligible minority consists of transferable dealers with repeat community, developer or institutional accounts or material recurring coordination and service operations. Public-company evidence confirms distribution to community operators and builder/developers and integrated installation and finance capabilities, but does not measure the eligible firm share.
e.change_evidence: A national dealer census matched to ownership and financial data, coding revenue by repeat community/developer accounts, installation and permitting services, one-time consumer home sales, parts, captive manufacturer activity and owner dependence.
caveats: There is no observed denominator of LMM firms, and the supplied n primitive is explicitly unavailable.
Large integrated manufacturers demonstrate business-model existence but may overstate service breadth relative to independent LMM dealers.

s5.bridge: Gallup measured five-year intentions across US owner-operated businesses, finding that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or ownership transfer, rather than completed qualifying control transfers among manufactured-home dealers. The base discounts intent for failed sales, internal reorganizations and nontransferable firms, while the upper range allows for active strategic consolidation evidenced by Champion's 2026 agreement for 11 Homes Direct retail locations. Integrated-manufacturer acquisition appetite may make eligible service-rich dealers more transferable than average, but one announced deal cannot establish a rate.
s5.rationale: Eligible dealers have customer relationships, licenses, locations and installation or permitting capabilities that strategic manufacturers can acquire. Nevertheless, planned exits do not always close, manufacturer concentration narrows the buyer set, and small dealership value can depend on owners and local relationships.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of dealer acquisitions, family or management transfers, failed listings and closures from specialist brokers, state dealer-license records, SBA lenders and manufacturer channel records, matched to the eligible-firm population.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry, includes gifts, and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
Champion's transaction is a strategic acquisition of locations and does not measure market-wide transfer frequency.

q.rationale: Dealers can initially retain savings in lead handling, document processing and coordination because customers pay a bundled product margin and ancillary service economics rather than hourly labor. Over five years, manufacturer negotiation, transparent home pricing, local dealer rivalry and buyer affordability pressure share savings through lower prices, better service or higher sales compensation. Savings tied to faster conversion and fewer errors should be stickier than savings in visible administrative fees.
q.change_evidence: Dealer-level five-year cohort P&Ls and contracts showing gross margin, discounts, manufacturer rebates, service fees, sales commissions and customer acquisition costs before and after documented automation, segmented by consumer and repeat B2B accounts.
caveats: No cited source observes pass-through of AI-enabled dealer savings.
Retention varies with manufacturer affiliation, local competition, financing conditions and whether installation or site work is separately contracted.

d5.bridge: Manufactured-home shipments and housing-market evidence measure product units and broad housing need, not five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand for the retained dealer service basket. MHI reported 9,270 US shipments in May 2025, with 44,927 year-to-date, while Urban Institute identifies inventory deficits, unaffordable prices, demand for lower-price housing and better manufactured-home affordability. The estimate translates those drivers into moderate service-quantity growth, but widens for interest-rate sensitivity, zoning, finance constraints and the possibility that shipments bypass independent dealers. Product-unit growth could overstate dealer-service growth if manufacturers integrate distribution.
d5.rationale: The structural need for lower-cost housing supports demand, and dealers coordinate a complex path from selection through placement. Zoning, land availability, chattel-finance limitations, rates and cyclical consumer confidence constrain realization, so the base is positive but the downside allows contraction.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year panel of dealer-delivered homes and service revenue at constant scope, separated by independent dealer, captive retail, direct-to-community and direct-to-developer channels, paired with approvals, loan applications and cancellations by market.
caveats: Shipment counts are product quantities and include channels outside the frozen dealer lens.
The Urban Institute report is national and directional and predates the latest interest-rate and policy environment.

o.bridge: Champion and Homes Direct describe a current service chain spanning home selection, financing, permitting, site preparation, transport, installation and set-up; this documents operator work but not the year-five quantity share requiring an independent LMM dealer. The estimate treats regulated, physical and multi-party coordination as persistent while allowing manufacturer-owned retail, direct community sales and standardized digital journeys to remove the independent dealer from some transactions. Evidence from large integrated firms may overstate integration benefits and understate the role of local independents.
o.rationale: A home can be discovered and configured digitally, but financing, permits, site readiness, delivery, installation, inspection and warranty handoffs still need an accountable coordinating operator in most included transactions. The operator may migrate to a manufacturer, builder or community, so not all durable demand remains with a firm of the lens's kind.
o.change_evidence: Channel-level transaction data from manufacturers and state title records showing the share of homes sold through independent dealers, captive retail, direct-to-community or fully direct channels, plus responsibility for permits, installation defects and warranty claims.
caveats: Public-company descriptions show capabilities, not a representative industry workflow distribution.
An accountable operator may remain necessary while the independent dealer is vertically displaced, which is specifically a loss under this lens.

narrative.executive_view: Service-rich manufactured-home dealers have a useful AI surface in sales, finance, permitting and coordination, but the industry code is fundamentally product retail and only a minority fits the repeat outsourced-service lens. Affordable-housing need supports demand, while channel integration by manufacturers threatens independent dealer durability.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can qualify leads, compare configurations, draft quotes and outreach, collect and check finance documents, prepare permit packets, track orders and schedule handoffs. Human accountability remains central for financial advice, fair-lending review, site assessment, permitting exceptions, negotiation, transport, installation and defect resolution.
narrative.value_capture: Bundled product margins can hide and retain early workflow savings, especially when faster response improves conversion or reduces errors. Savings will leak through manufacturer bargaining, competitive discounts, higher sales compensation and customer affordability pressure as tools diffuse.
narrative.firm_availability: The target is narrower than the NAICS population because one-time home retail is not a recurring outsourced service. Employer-owner transfer intent and recent manufacturer acquisitions indicate a live control market, but the eligible-firm count is missing and strategic buyers may favor scalable multi-location platforms.
narrative.demand_durability: Housing undersupply and the relative affordability of factory-built homes support five-year unit demand. Finance access, interest rates, zoning, land and siting constraints can block that demand, and direct manufacturer or community channels can grow without preserving an independent dealer.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainties are the unmeasured eligible-firm population, lack of an industry occupation mix, ancestor-level labor ratio, rate and credit sensitivity, local permitting variation, fair-lending and privacy exposure, installation liability, manufacturer concentration and vertical disintermediation.
narrative.principal_driver: Document-heavy financing and permitting workflows create implementable labor savings against a durable need for affordable housing and complex placement coordination.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most NAICS establishments are one-time durable-goods retailers, and manufacturers can absorb the service layer through captive retail and direct channels.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 459930 Manufactured (Mobile) Home Dealers
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily retailing new and/or used manufactured homes, parts and equipment.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Retail Salespersons
sources.supports: Lists customer-needs discovery, product recommendation, payment processing, inventory, sales records, contracts, trade-in estimates and arranging delivery, insurance, financing or service contracts among retail-sales tasks.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Loan Interviewers and Clerks
sources.supports: Lists 18 tasks including verifying applications, assembling closing documents, recording and submitting applications, maintaining files, checking collateral, preparing legal and government forms, calculating costs and conducting closings.

sources.title: Champion Homes 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Reports distribution through independent and company-owned retail, community operators and builder/developers; 72 active sales centers; installation and set-up construction services; trucking; and dealer floor-plan and consumer financing.

sources.title: Champion Homes Announces Agreement to Acquire Retail Locations from Homes Direct
sources.supports: Reports a May 2026 agreement to acquire assets representing 11 western US retail locations and describes Homes Direct support for permitting, financing, home selection and site preparation.

sources.title: Manufactured Housing Institute Monthly Economic Report: May 2025
sources.supports: Reports 9,270 US manufactured-home shipments in May 2025 and 44,927 year-to-date, with 42.5% single-section and 57.5% multi-section in May.

sources.title: Urban Institute: The Role of Manufactured Housing in Increasing the Supply of Affordable Housing
sources.supports: Finds that housing inventory deficits, unaffordable prices, growing demand for low-price housing, consumer willingness and better affordability support manufactured housing, while financing and zoning improvements are needed.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 survey of 1,264 US owner-operators found 14% overall and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; 8% overall planned to close.
